stimulus: Biologist Renata Okwuchi and her team have studied two subspecies of the cliff-breeding common swift that nest along the Iberian coast. One subspecies migrates across the Sahara each fall, while the other remains in Europe year-round. The researchers found that, owing to slight differences in feather barbule structure, the wings of migratory common swift males make a faint, distinctive whistling sound during high-speed flight, whereas those of nonmigratory males are nearly silent. Okwuchi and her team hypothesize that, over many generations, the whistle has been refined by sexual selection—females in the migratory subspecies preferentially mate with males whose feathers produce a higher-pitched whistle—rather than being a mere by-product of the feather shape.
stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Okwuchi and her team’s hypothesis?

answerOptions.content: The feathers located on the wings of migratory common swifts have a narrower barbule shape than those of nonmigratory swifts, which allows for more efficient flight.
Common swifts communicate different messages to each other depending on whether their feathers produce high-pitched or low-pitched sounds.
Over several generations, the whistle produced by the feathers of migratory male common swifts has grown progressively higher pitched relative to that of nonmigratory males.
The breeding habits of the migratory and nonmigratory common swifts have remained generally the same over several generations.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer because it provides the kind of generational evidence the hypothesis requires. If females in the migratory subspecies preferentially mate with higher-pitched-whistle males over time, sexual selection should drive the whistle to become progressively higher pitched in the migratory subspecies relative to the nonmigratory one. A documented generational rise in pitch is precisely this signature.

Choice A is incorrect because flight efficiency speaks to natural selection on aerodynamics, not to the sexual-selection mechanism the hypothesis invokes. Choice B is incorrect because it concerns communication content rather than mate choice, which is what the hypothesis specifies. Choice D is incorrect because static breeding habits provide no evidence of selection on the whistle pitch over time.
